text: welcome to this video from James Cook University Hospital my name is Joel Dunning and together with John Wallace we had this patient who required ligation of the thoracic duct we elected to use a VATS approach using a posterior approach rather than an anterior approach
You can just see in the video right now the oesophagus that we're grasping pulling anteriorly and we've opened up the pleura and the ozygous posteriorly and the vertebral column.
We're just using this rather elegant gadget to place a suture anteriorly and then posteriorly in order to reflect.

side the area in which the thoracic duct is located we're pulling the pleura anteriorly and you can just see the esophagus the longitudinal fibers just below it and now more posteriorly we're just placing a holding suture just to retract
As I say, we used a posterior approach.
The camera is in a port that's just in the posterior axillary line, fifth intercostal space, using the same approach that we use for posterior approach vats libectomy.
So now we've changed the camera to the inferior port and we're now investigating the space posterior to the oesophagus.
You can see the ozygous vein just curving round at the top of the picture to insert into the superior vena cava.
And then we've identified the thoracic duct just there.
It lies posterior to the oesophagus and just anterior to the ozygous vein.
We slung this to make ligating it easier.
And we, in fact, used clips.
This patient had a thyroid carcinoma and had a total thyroidectomy, an extensive cervical lymph node dissection, which caused a thoracic duct injury in the neck.
They had two reoperations attempting to close it in the neck which were both unsuccessful which is the reason we were asked to tie off this duct.
So here is the duct slung nicely and we've in fact clipped it with three clips inferiorly and three clips superiorly.

The patient had come to us after two weeks with a low fat diet which had failed to resolve it and the patient in fact did not have a chylothorax because it was all localised in the neck but the neck was swelling very severely.
And here is us just cutting the thoracic duct.
We felt we should take a segment out.
And here is the thoracic duct that we've removed.
And we sent this for histology.
And histology confirmed there was no muscular layer, and therefore, it was not a vessel.
And it was, in fact, the thoracic duct.
So that was the procedure.
It's then our routine to take a medius musculi needle and just inject local anaesthetic into each intercostal space.
thereby making sure that the patient was completely pain-free post-operatively.
Thank you very much for watching from all of us at James Cook University Hospital.
